metadata.title: Research Critical Thinking Modes with Claude: Which Tool for Which Problem

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The sequencing diagnosis is what I want to stress-test, because firing Critical mode before Generating would kill ideas before they exist. I want to see it break. Help me: (1) take writing a research paper and deliberately run the three modes out of order, evaluating evidence before any questions are generated, (2) show concretely what output degrades at each misordered stage, (3) contrast that with the correct Generating then Analytical then Critical sequence on the same starting prompt.

props.command: The sequencing diagnosis is what I want to stress-test, because firing Critical mode before Generating would kill ideas before they exist. I want to see it break. Help me: (1) take writing a research paper and deliberately run the three modes out of order, evaluating evidence before any questions are generated, (2) show concretely what output degrades at each misordered stage, (3) contrast that with the correct Generating then Analytical then Critical sequence on the same starting prompt.
props.segment: Research Critical Thinking Modes with Claude:

beats.narration_text: Creative, analytical, and critical thinking feel like synonyms until you need to use the right one at the right stage.

Three thinking modes: Generating and Creative — expanding possibilities. Decomposing and Analytical — breaking into parts. Evaluating and Critical — judging evidence and claims. Most struggling students are using only one mode — usually the wrong one for the stage they're in. A student generating thesis ideas when they should be evaluating evidence is not thinking badly — they're using the right tool in the wrong moment.
Ask Claude to describe the three thinking modes and apply all three to the same contradicting paper scenario.

props.command: claude "Describe three thinking modes from the college success framework: (1) Generating/Creative — what it is, when to use it; (2) Decomposing/Analytical — what it is, when to use it; (3) Evaluating/Critical — what it is, when to use it. For each, add one example of what NOT using it looks like academically. Then apply all three modes to: a student reads a research paper that contradicts their thesis. Show how each mode produces a different response."

beats.narration_text: The 3-row table and worked example showing how each mode produces a different response to the same contradicting paper.

props.code:
| Mode       | Definition          | When to use          | NOT using it         |
|------------|---------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Generating | Expand possibilities| Before you evaluate  | Jumping straight to  |
| /Creative  | brainstorm widely   | (Stage 1: diverge)   | judgment at Stage 1  |
| Decomposing| Break into parts;   | Before you evaluate  | Writing thesis before|
| /Analytical| map the structure   | (Stage 2: structure) | reading sources      |
| Evaluating | Judge evidence;     | After generating and | Evaluating ideas you |
| /Critical  | assess claims       | decomposing          | haven't generated    |

# Contradicting paper — three responses:
# Generating: "5 ways this paper could strengthen the thesis"
# Decomposing: map paper's claims and evidence structure
# Evaluating: compare methodology — sample size, controls

beats.narration_text: Three distinct responses to the contradicting paper: Creative mode produces 5 reframing possibilities — none dismiss the contradiction, they work with it. Analytical mode produces a claim map showing the paper's structure. Critical mode produces a methodology comparison: which study's sample size is larger, which controls for confounds. Three genuinely different outputs from the same input — the mode, not the information, determines the output.
Diagnose the sequencing: for writing a research paper from scratch, which mode fires first, second, third?

props.command: claude "For writing a research paper from scratch, prescribe the correct sequence of thinking modes: which fires first, second, third, and why? Show what goes wrong when a student starts with Critical mode at Stage 1."

beats.narration_text: Sequencing diagnosis: Stage 1 Generating — brainstorm 10 possible research questions, not find the right question. Stage 2 Analytical — decompose the strongest 3 into their parts: claim, evidence needed, scope. Stage 3 Critical — evaluate which claim is most defensible given available sources. The three modes form a natural order: expand, then structure, then evaluate. Students who go straight to Critical mode at Stage 1 before generating have nothing to evaluate.

The layer above the work matters more than the work itself. Generating 10 thesis statements, then decomposing the strongest, then evaluating the evidence is the correct sequence — and it is the opposite of how most students approach the assignment: start with evaluation, skip generation, never decompose. The mode determines the output; the sequence determines whether the output is useful.
Next: research the three communication failure modes that cost students the most.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The layer above the work matters more than the work itself. Generating 10 thesis statements, then decomposing the strongest, then evaluating the evidence is the correct sequence — and it is the opposite of how most students approach the assignment: start with evaluation, skip generation, never decompose. The mode determines the output; the sequence determines whether the output is useful. Next: research the three communication failure modes that cost students the most.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Research Critical Thinking Modes with Claude: Which Tool for Which Pro] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
